---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 11: More topics in linear unobserved effects models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 11](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-11-more-topics-in-linear-unobserved-effects-models/).

Textbook: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*, MIT Press.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

# Data

```{julia}
cornwell = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("cornwell"))
size(cornwell)
```

## Example 11.3 (cornwell.dta)

Dynamic unobserved-effects model estimated in first differences, with the twice-
and thrice-lagged level of `lcrmrte` used as instruments for the lagged
first difference (Wooldridge, p. 305).

```{julia}
#=
use cornwell, clear

tsset county year
       panel variable:  county, 1 to 197
        time variable:  year, 81 to 87

ivreg d.lcrmrte (d.l.lcrmrte = l2.lcrmrte l3.lcrmrte) if year ==87 | year==88

Instrumental variables (2SLS) regression

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =      90
-------------+------------------------------           F(  1,    88) =    0.18
       Model | -1.08714648     1 -1.08714648           Prob > F      =  0.6704
    Residual |  6.18074089    88  .070235692           R-squared     =       .
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =       .
       Total |  5.09359441    89  .057231398           Root MSE      =  .26502

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
D.lcrmrte    |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
lcrmrte      |
          LD |    .212428   .4973782     0.43   0.670    -.7760066    1.200863
_cons        |   .0646336   .0404458     1.60   0.114    -.0157439     .145011
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Instrumented:  LD.lcrmrte
Instruments:   L2.lcrmrte L3.lcrmrte
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

reg d.l.lcrmrte l2.lcrmrte l3.lcrmrte if  year ==87 | year==88

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =      90
-------------+------------------------------           F(  2,    87) =    3.95
       Model |  .283912416     2  .141956208           Prob > F      =  0.0228
    Residual |  3.12513763    87  .035921122           R-squared     =  0.0833
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.0622
       Total |  3.40905005    89  .038303933           Root MSE      =  .18953

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LD.lcrmrte   |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
lcrmrte      |
          L2 |  -.2616013   .0939729    -2.78   0.007    -.4483825     -.07482
          L3 |   .2598239   .0948714     2.74   0.007     .0712568     .448391
_cons        |   .0532431   .1287512     0.41   0.680    -.2026638      .30915
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

test l2.lcrmrte l3.lcrmrte

 ( 1)  L2.lcrmrte = 0
 ( 2)  L3.lcrmrte = 0

       F(  2,    87) =    3.95
            Prob > F =    0.0228
=#
```

### Building the `tsset` time-series operators

Stata's `tsset county year` makes `D.`, `L.`, `L2.`, `L3.` available. Julia has
no operator sugar, so we build the lagged/differenced columns explicitly.
The estimation sample `year == 87 | year == 88` is just `year == 87` (the panel
ends in 87), so each of the 90 counties contributes exactly one observation:

* `D.lcrmrte`    = `lcrmrte`(87) − `lcrmrte`(86)
* `LD.lcrmrte`   = `lcrmrte`(86) − `lcrmrte`(85)
* `L2.lcrmrte`   = `lcrmrte`(85)
* `L3.lcrmrte`   = `lcrmrte`(84)

```{julia}
DataFrames.sort!(cornwell, [:county, :year])
lc = Dict((r.county, r.year) => Float64(r.lcrmrte) for r in eachrow(cornwell))

cw87 = DataFrames.DataFrame(county = Int[], D_lcrmrte = Float64[],
                            LD_lcrmrte = Float64[],
                            L2_lcrmrte = Float64[], L3_lcrmrte = Float64[])
for c in sort(unique(cornwell.county))
    all(haskey(lc, (c, y)) for y in 84:87) || continue
    push!(cw87, (c,
                 lc[(c, 87)] - lc[(c, 86)],
                 lc[(c, 86)] - lc[(c, 85)],
                 lc[(c, 85)],
                 lc[(c, 84)]))
end
DataFrames.nrow(cw87)
```

90 observations, matching Stata's `Number of obs = 90`.

### 2SLS (Stata `ivreg`)

```{julia}
iv = stata_ivregress_2sls(cw87, :D_lcrmrte,
                          :LD_lcrmrte => [:L2_lcrmrte, :L3_lcrmrte];
                          robust = false, first = false)
```

**Comparison with the published Stata output.** The point estimates and standard
errors reproduce Stata exactly:

| coefficient | Stata | Julia |
|---|---|---|
| `LD.lcrmrte` | .212428 (.4973782) | .2124280 (.4973782) |
| `_cons` | .0646336 (.0404458) | .0646336 (.0404458) |

Root MSE `.26502` also matches. The only display difference is the test
metric: Stata's old `ivreg` reports a small-sample `F(1, 88) = 0.18 / p = 0.6704`
with `t` ratios, while `stata_ivregress_2sls` follows the modern
`ivregress 2sls` convention and reports `Wald chi2(1) = 0.18 / p = 0.6693` with
`z` ratios. Same statistic, different reference distribution, hence the p-value
in the third decimal. Stata's R-squared is reported as missing (`.`) because the
model sum of squares is negative; `FixedEffectModels` prints the (negative)
value −0.2134 instead.

### First-stage regression (Stata `reg`)

```{julia}
first_stage = stata_regress(cw87; y = :LD_lcrmrte,
                            x = [:L2_lcrmrte, :L3_lcrmrte])
```

**Comparison.** Exact match on every reported quantity: `L2 = -.2616013`
(SE `.0939729`), `L3 = .2598239` (SE `.0948714`), `_cons = .0532431`
(SE `.1287512`), `F(2, 87) = 3.95`, `Prob > F = 0.0228`, `R-squared = 0.0833`,
`Root MSE = .18953`.

### Joint significance of the instruments (Stata `test`)

```{julia}
stata_test(first_stage, ["L2_lcrmrte", "L3_lcrmrte"])
```

**Comparison.** `F(2, 87) = 3.95`, `Prob > F = 0.0228` — identical to the
published Stata `test` output. With an F of only about 4 on two instruments,
the instruments are weak, which is exactly the point the textbook makes about
the very imprecise 2SLS estimate (SE ≈ 0.50 on a coefficient of 0.21).
